Among the replies which Philip Barron received (circa, 1834) with
reference to his projects for the cultivation of the Irish language was %
one from John M’Hale, Archbishop of Tuam, stating that, ‘“ We have '
some excellent Irish scholars: but books or manuscripts are scarce. The ]
-~ Rev. Mr. Lyons, P.P. (his address is Belmullet), understands particu-
larly well our national language, as also the Rev. Mn. Fennelly (Easky,
Ballina), who resides in the immediate vicinity of Lecan, so famous ana
interesting for the book of Irish Ammnals which takes its name from =
that place.”
The Rev. J. P. Lyons, P.P., Kilmore, Erris, Belmullet, also wrote
himself to Barron regretting that the poverty of his parish and the 2 i
arduous nature of his duties prevented him from co-operating in Bar- : :
ron’s work. ‘‘ Would to God,” he wrote, ‘‘ that I had some such mis- , |
sion near you, or some situation in a college or seminary ; I could then 3

devote my mind and any energies I possess to the language and anti-
‘quities of our ancient country, and render you, perhaps, some servioe
towards achieving your glorious object.

“The Rev. Mr. Loftus, P.P., of Dunmore, and the Rev. Mr.

Ronayne, of St. Jerlath’s College, Tuam, are good Irish scholars. Dr. Ehs
M’Hiale also reads and writes it.”’ e

e Dean Lyons is mentioned in 1839 #n a R.I.A. MS. (23 H. 34), cited
i_ ~ In “Gadelica.” (Vol. L, p. 115.) ‘

- lin, 1849) contains an appreciatory letter dated ‘* Lyonsport, 18th May,
1841, from J. P. Lyons, D.D., R.C. Dean of Killala, and P.P. of Kil-
} | ‘more, Firris.

g an autograph of the first part of the Ammals of the Four Masters.
On the lst Jume, 1842, and again on the lst July. 1843, Dean Lyong

wrote from Rome to Drs. Todd and O’ Donovan, enclosing description
and pemcil tracings of the MSS. These tracings were gone over in ink

by Eugene O’Curry, pasted into a book specially made for the purpose,
and deposited in the Royal Ivish Academy. O’Curry’s catalo gue of the .
MSS., based on Dean Lyons’ notes, was published without permissiop

or acknowledgment in Rev. J. Donovam’s ‘‘Ancient and Modern Rome"
(1843). Upon Dean Lyons’ return to Ireland, Dr. Todd corresiponded
with him regarding the purchase of the MSS. for Ireland, but the death
of the Dean and the famine caused the suspension of the negotiations,

About 1872 the MSS. were transferred from St. Isidore’s to Dublin, =

where they are now in the care of the Franciscans on Merchant’s Quay.

Dean Lyons died on 15th March, 1845, at his residence, Binghams-

town, Co. Mayo, and the ‘‘ Miayo Constitution’ records that * The

deceased was a distinguished member of the Roman Catholic church, a
man of high character, of great abilities, much liberality of mind, and
a warm and sincere friend. His death will be d eeply regretted by a
large circle who admired his great and varied acquirements and amiable
disposition.’’

I have not been able to discover any work published over his name,
but perhaps these few particulars may stimulate interest in the search
for his unpublished history of Erris. (Vide, R.I.A., Proo. 1895, p. 649.)
The Very Rev. Camon Dalton, M.R.I.A., who is engaged in compiling
the history of the Archdiocese of Tuam, may be able to supply further
biographical information from the records at his disposal.

The library of the late Very Rev. Dean Lyons, D.D. (of Bin alhams-
town, Belmullet, and Kilmore, Erris, P.P.), was sold by auction by
Charles Sharpe, at 31, Anglesea Stneet, Dublin, on 27th August, 1849,
and following two days. The Catalogue (60 + 4 octavo pages) printed
by Webb and Chapman, Gt. Brunswick Street, Dublin (1845), enumei-
ated 952 items, of which Nos. 587-595 were MSS. mainly in Latin, and
Nos. 825-845 were prints, etc. |

Some rare Irish books were included such as Matthew Kennedy's
tramslation into Irish of the Spiritual Rose (Monaghan, 1819), and a
beautifully writben MS. (in Trish) of an Ode to Heaven and an Ode to
Hell, wetc.

Two wvery remarkable lots consisted of 66 copies of the Rev.
Bonaventure 0’Hussey’s ‘ An Teagasg Oriosdaidhe »’ (Rome, 1707), and

978 copies of the Rev. IFrancis O’Molloy’s ** Lucerna Fidelium *’ (Romze,
1676.) John O’Daly may have acquired some of the stock of these two
Irish Catechisms, as he olfered single copies for sale at very moderate
prices, ©.2., 1 1867 he catalogued a copy of O’Hussey’s Catechism at
ihree shillings.

About twenty years ago a large stock of O’Molloy’s Catechism was
discovered on the shelves of Messrs. Hodges & Figgis, of Dublin. The
books had evidently been overlooked for a long time, and the firm
have mever succeeded in finding any record of how they had been ac-
quired. The greater part of the stock has since been disposed of at
about five shillings a copy. There can now be but little doubt that
they were acquired at Dean Iqons’ sale, and he must have obtained
them during his stay in Rome, probably at the College of the Propa-
garda, where they were originally printed with a special fount of Irish

type. SEAMUS UA CASAIDE.

Calvert in January and February, 1650. Recenitly, a periodical entitled
an ‘‘ Account of the Chief Occurremces in Ireland,” bearing date 1639
has been discovered in the library of Worcester College by Mr. E, p
McC. Dix, and appears to have been equally ephemeral. Mr. Dix hgs
also brought to light ‘* Mercurius Hibernicus,” heensed in 1663 (No, 1
13-20 January), printed for Samuel Dancer, bookseller, ““ at the sign of
the Horse Shoe,” in Castle Street, Dublin, and apparently limited to
ten mumbers. The ‘‘ Dublin News Letter” (No. 17, 26th August) fol.
lowed in 1685. In the year 1690 a ‘‘ Gazette’’ is said to have heey
issued by James II., im Dublin; but no copy is known to exist. The
hitherto unknown ‘* Dublin Intelligence, Published by Authority,” and

~printed by Joseph Ray on ‘‘ Colledge ” Green, for Robert Thornton (No,

1, 2-9 February, 1691) was certainly issued in that year. In 1692 a
paper which, also, has never heen noticed, entitled ‘‘ Votes of the House

of Commons in Ireland, Licensed and Entered According, to Order,”
was printed in Dublin by William Norman and reprinted in London by
R. Roberts (No. 1, 5th October, 1692). ‘‘Pue’s Occurrences’ was
1ssued at the end of 1703, and lasted for some years. Few numbers
exist. The official *‘ Dublin Gazette ’’ began in 1706, and by 1715 the
Irish Press ajpipears to have been well in view with the ‘“‘Dublin News-let-

ter’’ and the “Flying Post,” or ‘“Postboy,”” both printed by E. Watersin
